_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2016 ACR revised fibromyalgia criteria (Wolfe), AAPT 2019 diagnostic criteria (Arnold), 2017 EULAR fibromyalgia recommendations (Macfarlane), pivotal RCTs for duloxetine (Arnold 2004), milnacipran (Clauw 2008) and pregabalin (Crofford 2005), and CDC chronic pain prevalence data. Low-dose naltrexone evidence from Younger 2013 RCT.

h1Title: Fibromyalgia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Fibromyalgia is a central nervous system pain-processing disorder, not joint or muscle damage, in which the brain and spinal cord amplify ordinary sensory signals into chronic widespread pain. It affects roughly 2-4% of adults globally, with a strong female predominance of 7-9 to 1 and onset most often between ages 30 and 55. Patients describe persistent aching across all four quadrants of the body alongside profound fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive symptoms known as fibro fog. Standard imaging and inflammatory blood work are normal, which historically delayed diagnosis by an average of 5 years, but the 2016 ACR criteria and 2019 AAPT framework now allow confident clinical diagnosis. Treatment combines graded exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and three FDA-approved medications — duloxetine, milnacipran, and pregabalin — with meaningful symptom reduction in roughly 30-50% of treated patients.

keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio 7-9:1 under 2010 ACR criteria; equalizes somewhat with 2016 criteria
keyStats.avgAge: Peak diagnosis age 30-55; mean delay from symptom onset to diagnosis 2.3-5 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200-400 million people worldwide; commonest cause of chronic generalized musculoskeletal pain

definition: Fibromyalgia (ICD-10: M79.7) is a chronic central sensitization syndrome defined by widespread musculoskeletal pain lasting at least three months together with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction in the absence of tissue inflammation or structural damage. The underlying biology is augmented central pain processing: functional MRI shows heightened activation of pain-processing regions (insula, anterior cingulate, somatosensory cortex) at stimulus intensities that healthy controls perceive as non-painful, and cerebrospinal fluid studies (Russell 1994) demonstrate elevated substance P and glutamate alongside reduced descending serotonergic and noradrenergic inhibition. The condition is no longer classified as a rheumatic disease in the traditional sense but as a nociplastic pain disorder under the 2017 IASP framework, sitting alongside irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis, temporomandibular disorder, and chronic tension-type headache. Severity ranges from intermittent symptoms manageable with lifestyle measures to disabling pain with major functional impairment. Rheumatologists most often establish the diagnosis, but management is shared across primary care, pain medicine, physiatry, psychiatry, and sleep medicine because no single specialty addresses all symptom domains.

typesClassification.description: Develops without an identifiable triggering condition. Represents roughly 60-70% of cases and typically has a strong family-history component.

Develops in the setting of another chronic disease such as rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, or osteoarthritis. Around 20-30% of patients with inflammatory arthritis meet criteria for concomitant fibromyalgia.
Onset within 6-12 months of a physical trigger such as motor vehicle accident, surgery, or severe infection. Whiplash injuries are associated with a 10-13 fold increased risk.
Diagnosed in adolescents, typically ages 13-15 with female predominance. Prognosis is more favorable than adult-onset disease, though up to 50% continue to meet criteria into adulthood.

primarySymptoms: Widespread pain affecting both sides of the body, above and below the waist, persisting for at least three months and often described as a deep aching, burning, or throbbing in muscles and soft tissue.
Profound fatigue that is disproportionate to activity and is not relieved by rest, present on most days and rated moderate-to-severe by roughly 90% of patients.

Unrefreshing sleep with frequent awakenings, difficulty falling asleep, and waking exhausted even after 8-9 hours in bed — polysomnography typically shows intrusion of alpha waves into deep sleep stages.
Cognitive symptoms known as fibro fog, including reduced working memory, slowed processing speed, word-finding difficulty, and impaired concentration that worsen with pain and fatigue.
Heightened sensitivity to pressure, temperature, sound, light, and odors, with everyday stimuli that healthy people tolerate easily perceived as painful or overwhelming.
Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes or longer in 75-80% of patients, often described as gel-like and easing with gentle movement rather than remaining constant.
Headaches affecting 50-60% of patients, most often tension-type but with overlapping migraine features and clear sensitivity to triggers such as poor sleep or weather changes.
Functional gastrointestinal symptoms including alternating constipation and diarrhea, bloating, and abdominal cramping — irritable bowel syndrome is diagnosed in 40-70% of fibromyalgia patients.
Mood symptoms including anxiety in approximately 45% and depression in approximately 30% of patients; these are bidirectional with pain rather than the cause of fibromyalgia.
Numbness, tingling, or pins-and-needles sensations (paresthesias) in the limbs without an objective neurological deficit on examination.
earlyWarningSigns: Aching that begins in one region (often the neck and shoulders) and gradually spreads to involve both sides of the body over months
Persistent unrefreshing sleep and morning exhaustion despite adequate time in bed, often preceding pain by 1-2 years
Heightened sensitivity to pressure such as bra straps, watches, or seat belts feeling painful at normal tension
Cognitive slowing and word-finding difficulty in a previously sharp thinker, especially during pain flares
Onset of widespread pain within 6-12 months of a major physical or psychological stressor (motor vehicle accident, surgery, severe infection, bereavement)
emergencySigns: New focal neurological deficit such as one-sided weakness, facial droop, vision loss, or sudden loss of coordination — these are not fibromyalgia and require urgent evaluation for stroke or demyelinating disease
Severe acute joint swelling with warmth, redness, and inability to bear weight — fibromyalgia does not cause objective joint swelling and another diagnosis must be sought
Unintended weight loss exceeding 10% of body weight, persistent fevers, night sweats, or new lymphadenopathy — these are red flags for malignancy, infection, or inflammatory disease
Suicidal ideation or active plan — depression and chronic pain raise suicide risk in fibromyalgia roughly 3-fold; an immediate mental-health contact is required
Severe headache that is sudden and worst-ever, especially with neck stiffness, confusion, or fever — assess for subarachnoid hemorrhage or meningitis rather than attributing to fibromyalgia

causes.cause: Central sensitization and altered pain processing
causes.description: The dominant mechanism. Repeated or sustained nociceptive input rewires spinal cord and brain circuits so that low-intensity signals are amplified into pain. Functional imaging shows exaggerated activation of the insula, anterior cingulate, and somatosensory cortex at stimulus levels that are non-painful for controls.

Cerebrospinal fluid studies show elevated substance P, glutamate, and nerve growth factor with reduced serotonin and norepinephrine metabolites. This combination shifts the balance from descending pain inhibition toward facilitation, and explains why drugs that boost serotonin and norepinephrine reduce symptoms.

First-degree relatives of patients have an 8-fold increased risk. Candidate-gene studies implicate variants in catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT), the serotonin transporter (5-HTTLPR), and dopamine D4 receptor genes. No single gene is responsible — fibromyalgia is polygenic.

Roughly 20-30% of cases follow an identifiable physical trigger including motor vehicle collision (especially whiplash), major surgery, severe infection (post-viral, Lyme disease, COVID-19), or childbirth. The trigger is thought to overwhelm an already vulnerable pain-processing system.

causes.cause: Psychological stress and adverse childhood experiences
causes.description: Severe or sustained stress, post-traumatic stress disorder, and adverse childhood experiences raise fibromyalgia risk approximately 2-3 fold. The mechanism involves dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and altered autonomic tone, not psychogenic pain.

Alpha-wave intrusion into non-REM sleep is a recognized polysomnography pattern in fibromyalgia. Sleep deprivation alone reliably reproduces tender points and fatigue in healthy volunteers, suggesting that sleep disturbance is both a symptom and a perpetuator.

causes.cause: Small fiber neuropathy in a subset
causes.description: Skin biopsy studies (Oaklander 2013) show reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density in 30-50% of fibromyalgia patients. Whether this represents a discrete subgroup or a peripheral contributor to central sensitization remains an open research question.

riskFactors.description: Women are diagnosed 7-9 times more often than men under the 2010 ACR criteria; the gap narrows with the 2016 criteria but remains roughly 3:1. Hormonal modulation of pain processing is one suspected reason.
Peak incidence falls in middle age, though new diagnoses occur from adolescence through the eighth decade. Onset is uncommon under age 18 and rare under age 12.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of fibromyalgia or chronic pain
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives have an 8-fold increased risk. Family clustering exceeds what genetics alone predicts and likely includes shared environment and learned pain behaviors.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing chronic pain or rheumatic disease
riskFactors.description: Around 20-30% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, or ankylosing spondylitis develop concomitant fibromyalgia. Recognizing the overlap matters because escalating biologic therapy does not treat the fibromyalgia component.

riskFactors.factor: Sleep disorders such as obstructive sleep apnea and restless legs syndrome
riskFactors.description: Both are independent risk factors for the development and maintenance of fibromyalgia. Treating untreated sleep apnea reduces fibromyalgia symptom severity in observational series.
Patients with four or more adverse childhood experiences have roughly a 3-fold higher fibromyalgia risk. The relationship is mediated through HPA-axis dysregulation rather than psychogenic causation.
Whiplash injury raises fibromyalgia incidence 10-13 fold within a year of injury (Buskila 1997). Major surgery and severe infection produce smaller but measurable signals.
Obesity is both a risk factor and a severity amplifier. Each 5-unit rise in BMI increases symptom severity scores; modest weight loss improves pain and function in trial settings.

Low cardiovascular fitness measurably worsens central pain processing and is one of the few risk factors that responds quickly to intervention — graded aerobic exercise reduces symptoms within 8-12 weeks.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: roughly 4 million adults (CDC NHIS 2012 estimate of 2.0%)
Female predominance ranges from 7-9:1 under 2010 ACR criteria to roughly 3:1 with the 2016 update — the older criteria over-weighted tender points which women report more readily
Prevalence rises with age, peaking in the sixth decade in population studies; childhood-onset disease accounts for under 5% of cases
Higher rates reported in lower-income populations and in patients with limited access to care, partly reflecting cumulative stress and delayed treatment of comorbid conditions
Black and Hispanic patients in the US are diagnosed later and offered fewer evidence-based therapies than white patients despite similar prevalence (Schmaling 2017)
diagnosisOverview: Fibromyalgia is a clinical diagnosis. There is no blood test, imaging study, or biopsy that confirms it; testing is used to exclude conditions that mimic it. Most current practice uses the 2016 ACR revised criteria (Wolfe), which require widespread pain (Widespread Pain Index of 7 or higher plus Symptom Severity Score of 5 or higher, or WPI 4-6 plus SSS of 9 or higher), pain in at least four of five regions, duration of three months or more, and symptoms not better explained by another disorder. The 2019 AAPT criteria (Arnold) offer a parallel framework requiring multi-site pain (at least 6 of 9 sites), moderate or greater sleep disturbance or fatigue, and at least three months of symptoms. Tender-point examination, which dominated the 1990 ACR criteria, is no longer required, though many clinicians still perform it for confirmatory value. The workup focuses on excluding alternatives: complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid stimulating hormone, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, creatine kinase, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 are standard. Antinuclear antibody is checked only if there are features suggesting connective tissue disease, since up to a third of healthy women have a positive ANA at low titer. Sleep studies are arranged when obstructive sleep apnea is suspected. The diagnostic delay remains substantial — averaging 2.3 to 5 years in international cohorts — driven by intermittent presentation, normal investigations, and clinician unfamiliarity. A confident early diagnosis given to the patient is itself therapeutic and reduces unnecessary repeat testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical assessment with 2016 ACR criteria (Widespread Pain Index and Symptom Severity Score)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic tool. Calculates WPI based on the number of painful body regions in the past week and SSS for fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive symptoms, and somatic burden. A self-administered questionnaire that requires no laboratory testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-minute clinic conversation and questionnaire. The clinician maps painful regions on a body diagram and asks structured questions about sleep, fatigue, and cognition over the prior week. No needles or imaging.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, metabolic panel, TSH, ESR, CRP, CK, vitamin D, vitamin B12
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes anemia, hypothyroidism, inflammatory disease, myopathy, and vitamin deficiencies that can mimic fibromyalgia symptoms. Results are normal in primary fibromyalgia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw. Most labs return results within 24-48 hours. Fasting is generally not required for these tests.

diagnosticTests.test: Tender-point examination (digital palpation of 18 defined sites)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Optional under the 2016 ACR criteria. Historically required 11 of 18 tender points but the count has limited reproducibility and underestimates fibromyalgia in men. Useful for ongoing clinical impression rather than primary diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician applies thumb pressure of approximately 4 kg (enough to blanch the nail bed) at specific points across the neck, shoulders, chest, elbows, hips, buttocks, and knees. Each point is rated as painful or not.

diagnosticTests.test: Antinuclear antibody (ANA), rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP — when indicated
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for patients with features suggesting lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, or another connective tissue disease — joint swelling, photosensitive rash, alopecia, oral ulcers, Raynaud phenomenon. Routine ordering produces more false positives than diagnoses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests added to the initial draw. Mild ANA positivity at 1:80 titer is common in healthy women and does not change a fibromyalgia diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Indicated when there are clinical features of obstructive sleep apnea (loud snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime hypersomnolence), restless legs syndrome, or periodic limb movements. Treating undiagnosed sleep apnea improves fibromyalgia symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Either an overnight study in a sleep laboratory with EEG, ECG, oximetry, and respiratory monitoring, or a home sleep apnea test for selected patients. Results are typically reviewed 1-2 weeks later.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy for small fiber neuropathy — research and selected cases
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies intraepidermal nerve fiber density. Roughly 30-50% of fibromyalgia patients show reduced density. Not part of standard diagnosis but considered when peripheral neuropathy features are prominent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3 mm punch biopsy from the distal calf and thigh under local anesthetic. Heals with a small scar. Results from specialty labs take 4-6 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Effective fibromyalgia care is multimodal and prioritizes non-drug treatment first. The 2017 EULAR recommendations (Macfarlane) make graded aerobic and strengthening exercise the strong universal first step, supported by the largest body of randomized evidence of any single intervention. Patient education, cognitive behavioral therapy, and good sleep hygiene form the second pillar and are appropriate from the day of diagnosis. Pharmacotherapy is layered on for patients with moderate-to-severe symptoms not controlled by non-drug measures. Three medications hold FDA approval for fibromyalgia in the United States — pregabalin, duloxetine, and milnacipran — each studied in pivotal multicenter trials with 30-50% achieving a clinically meaningful 30% pain reduction at three months. Amitriptyline at 10-50 mg at night remains first-line internationally despite lacking US licensing, with Cochrane meta-analysis showing comparable response to the licensed agents. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are less effective than dual-acting agents and are reserved for patients with prominent comorbid depression. Tramadol can help selected patients short term but is not recommended long term given dependence risk and the better risk-benefit profile of the licensed agents. Strong opioids are explicitly recommended against by every modern guideline (ACR, EULAR, NICE, CDC) — they offer minimal pain benefit, worsen central sensitization, and add risks of dependence, hyperalgesia, and death. Treat-to-target principles do not yet exist for fibromyalgia in the way they do for rheumatoid arthritis or gout; instead, response is assessed by clinically meaningful improvement in pain, fatigue, sleep, and functioning measured at 3-month intervals. Medication is started at low dose, titrated slowly, and stopped if it has not produced meaningful benefit by 3 months — chronic prescribing of an ineffective drug is one of the commonest avoidable harms in fibromyalgia care. Emerging therapies including low-dose naltrexone (Younger 2013), transcranial magnetic stimulation, and cannabidiol products are being studied; evidence is promising but not yet sufficient to displace established first-line treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (30 mg daily for one week, then 60 mg daily; max 120 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: A serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor licensed by the FDA for fibromyalgia in 2008. Restores descending inhibitory pain pathways. Useful when depression or generalized anxiety coexist. Common side effects include nausea, dry mouth, and constipation in the first 2 weeks; persistent insomnia in 10-15%.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% of patients achieve at least 30% pain reduction at 12 weeks versus 32% on placebo (Arnold 2004, Lunn 2014 Cochrane review).

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (75 mg twice daily; titrate to 150-225 mg twice daily; max 450 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: A calcium-channel alpha-2-delta ligand licensed by the FDA for fibromyalgia in 2007 — the first such approval. Reduces glutamate release in the central nervous system. Helps pain, sleep, and anxiety. Drowsiness, dizziness, weight gain, and peripheral edema are dose-limiting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Around 40% achieve 30% pain reduction at 8 weeks versus 28% on placebo (Crofford 2005); modest dose-dependent improvement in sleep quality.

medicalTreatments.name: Milnacipran (50 mg twice daily; titrate to 100 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: An SNRI with relatively greater norepinephrine activity, FDA-approved for fibromyalgia in 2009. Often chosen when fatigue and cognitive symptoms predominate. Watch for nausea, sweating, palpitations, and blood pressure rise on titration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Around 35-40% achieve combined responder criteria (pain reduction plus global improvement) versus 25% placebo (Clauw 2008 MLN-MD-02).

medicalTreatments.name: Amitriptyline (10-25 mg at bedtime, titrate to 50 mg as tolerated)
medicalTreatments.description: A low-dose tricyclic that improves sleep depth and reduces pain via descending serotonergic and noradrenergic pathways. Not FDA-licensed for fibromyalgia but recommended in EULAR 2017 and NICE chronic primary pain guidance. Anticholinergic side effects and weight gain are common.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 35% achieve substantial benefit at 6-8 weeks; effect size comparable to duloxetine in network meta-analysis (Hauser 2013).

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclobenzaprine (5-10 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: Structurally related to tricyclics; improves sleep architecture and reduces morning stiffness. Useful in patients who cannot tolerate amitriptyline. Sedation, dry mouth, and dizziness are the main side effects; avoid in older adults due to falls risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain and sleep improvement in 30-40% of patients (Tofferi 2004 meta-analysis); fewer studies than duloxetine or pregabalin.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose naltrexone (1-4.5 mg at bedtime)
medicalTreatments.description: An opioid antagonist that at low doses modulates microglial activation in the central nervous system. Off-label and not FDA-approved for fibromyalgia. Generally well tolerated; vivid dreams and mild insomnia are the main early side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Around 30% report meaningful pain reduction in small randomized trials (Younger 2013); larger phase III trials are ongoing.

medicalTreatments.name: Tramadol (37.5-50 mg every 6 hours as needed; short course only)
medicalTreatments.description: A weak opioid with additional serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. Considered when first-line agents fail and severe pain limits activity, but limited to short courses due to dependence risk and serotonergic interactions. Avoid in patients also taking SSRIs or SNRIs without close monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 30% in roughly 35% of patients in short-term trials; no robust long-term efficacy data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tai chi (60-minute sessions twice weekly)
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trial (Wang 2010, 2018) showed tai chi produced greater symptom improvement than aerobic exercise alone at 24 weeks. Combines low-impact movement, breathing, and mindfulness — well tolerated across fitness levels.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (8-week MBSR course)
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analysis (Lauche 2013) shows moderate effect sizes for pain and quality of life. Best evidence for patients with prominent anxiety or sleep disturbance. Free MBSR curricula are available through several university programs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture (10-12 sessions over 8 weeks)
alternativeTreatments.description: Cochrane review (Deare 2013) shows modest short-term pain improvement, larger when combined with exercise. Effects fade within 6 months without continued sessions. Useful as adjunct, not replacement, for medication.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain, when an inflammatory or autoimmune condition cannot be confidently excluded, when there is concomitant rheumatic disease driving part of the symptom picture, or when first-line treatments at adequate doses have failed. Pain medicine, physiatry, sleep medicine, and mental health specialists each add value depending on which symptom domain dominates. Well-managed fibromyalgia without diagnostic uncertainty can be followed effectively in primary care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for clinicians who explicitly recognize fibromyalgia as a central nervous system pain disorder, who treat it with a structured combination of exercise, behavioral therapy, and evidence-based medication, and who do not prescribe long-term opioids or repeat unnecessary investigations. Continuity matters — a doctor who knows your baseline detects flares and side effects faster. Ask about their approach to graded exercise progression and whether they refer to local cognitive behavioral therapy programs.
preventionStrategies: Treat sleep disorders early — diagnose and address obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, and chronic insomnia before pain becomes widespread
Maintain regular aerobic exercise at moderate intensity for at least 150 minutes per week — sedentary deconditioning is one of the few modifiable upstream factors
Manage psychological stress and treat post-traumatic stress disorder, anxiety, and depression with evidence-based therapy rather than waiting for them to resolve on their own

Address chronic pain promptly when it appears in any one region — regional pain that lingers beyond expected healing should trigger early multimodal management rather than escalating imaging and procedures
Maintain a healthy weight — each unit reduction in BMI correlates with measurable improvement in pain processing scores
lifestyleModifications: Build a graded aerobic exercise habit, starting with 5-10 minutes of low-impact movement (walking, swimming, cycling, stationary bike) daily and adding 10% per week until reaching 30 minutes most days
Add resistance training twice weekly at modest intensity — improves muscle endurance and reduces pain without provoking flares when started at low load
Adopt consistent sleep timing with the same bed and wake times daily, dim light in the evening, and a wind-down routine that avoids screens for the final hour
Pace activity to avoid the boom-and-bust pattern — set realistic daily goals, rest before exhaustion, and break large tasks into shorter intervals
Practice a daily mindfulness or relaxation routine of 10-20 minutes — body scan, breath work, or guided imagery — to dampen sympathetic overdrive
Limit caffeine after midday and alcohol within 3 hours of bed, both of which fragment sleep and worsen next-day pain and fatigue
Keep a simple symptom diary that tracks sleep, exercise, mood, and pain — patterns help identify personal triggers and demonstrate gradual improvement
Communicate openly with family, employer, and clinician about good and bad days to set expectations and reduce social isolation

recommended: A Mediterranean eating pattern with vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, fish, and olive oil — small trials show modest pain and fatigue improvement
Vitamin D-containing foods and supplementation if serum 25-OH vitamin D is below 30 ng/mL — deficiency is common in fibromyalgia and correction reduces pain scores
Adequate protein spread across meals (20-30 g per meal) to support muscle maintenance during graded exercise
Magnesium-rich foods (leafy greens, nuts, legumes, whole grains) — observational association with lower pain scores; supplementation evidence remains modest
Regular hydration with water as the primary fluid throughout the day
avoid: Ultra-processed foods high in added sugar and refined carbohydrate — promote systemic inflammation and worsen sleep
Excessive caffeine, particularly after midday, which fragments sleep and amplifies next-day pain
Alcohol in the evening, which suppresses deep sleep stages already disrupted in fibromyalgia
Restrictive elimination diets without clinical guidance — they rarely help and often worsen nutrition and quality of life
Aspartame and monosodium glutamate in patients who report symptom flares with these additives, although controlled-trial evidence is limited

exerciseGuidelines: Graded aerobic exercise is the single most evidence-based treatment for fibromyalgia. Start at the level you can sustain without a 24-48 hour flare — for many patients this is 5-10 minutes of walking or pool exercise — and increase duration by roughly 10% per week before increasing intensity. Aquatic exercise is particularly well tolerated. Add light strength training twice weekly after 6-8 weeks of aerobic adaptation. The aim over 6-12 months is 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly plus two resistance sessions. Brief, predictable flares during early progression are expected and do not mean exercise is harmful; they shrink as fitness improves.
dailyManagement: Take medication at the same time each day to keep blood levels steady — pregabalin and duloxetine both work better with consistent dosing than as-needed use
Plan high-priority tasks for periods of typical lower pain (often mid-morning to early afternoon) and protect those windows from low-value commitments
Use heat (warm shower, heating pad) for stiffness and ice for localized flares — both are safe, low-cost, and patient-controlled
Practice 5-10 minutes of breath work or guided relaxation before bed to ease the transition to sleep
Keep a short, written flare plan — what you will do for the next 48 hours including reduced activity, hydration, sleep, and gentle movement — so decisions are pre-made when pain is worst
prognosis: Long-term outcomes depend largely on whether patients receive an early confident diagnosis, structured exercise, and access to evidence-based medication. With multimodal treatment, roughly 25% of patients enter remission and another 30-50% achieve clinically meaningful symptom reduction sustained over years. Total functional recovery is realistic for many, particularly younger patients with shorter symptom duration before diagnosis. Predictors of better outcome include earlier diagnosis, higher baseline activity, absence of severe depression, and engagement with non-drug therapy. Predictors of poorer outcome include high baseline symptom severity, long delay to diagnosis, comorbid major depression or PTSD, ongoing opioid use, and disability claims tied to symptom persistence. Fibromyalgia does not shorten life expectancy on average, but it raises suicide risk roughly 3-fold and is associated with higher cardiovascular event rates linked partly to physical inactivity. The trajectory is not progressive in a structural sense — there is no joint or muscle damage — and remissions of months to years are common with sustained treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Patients who start graded exercise, sleep restoration, and a first-line medication typically begin to notice improvement at 6-8 weeks, with clinically meaningful change at 12 weeks. Pain and fatigue often respond before cognitive symptoms, which can take 4-6 months to clear. Sustained improvement requires sustained treatment — discontinuing exercise or medication produces symptom return within 1-3 months in most patients. Setbacks during stressful life events are expected and rarely undo the underlying progress when treatment is resumed promptly.
complications: Severe functional disability with loss of employment — affects roughly 25-30% of patients in longitudinal cohorts when treatment is delayed or inadequate
Major depression, occurring in about 30% of patients lifetime, with elevated suicide risk (roughly 3-fold) requiring active screening and treatment
Opioid dependence and opioid-induced hyperalgesia when chronic opioids are prescribed — central sensitization worsens, pain scores rise over time, and discontinuation is medically difficult
Cardiovascular and metabolic disease from prolonged physical inactivity, including increased obesity, type 2 diabetes, and ischemic heart disease
Iatrogenic harm from repeated unnecessary investigations and procedures including spinal injections and surgeries that do not address central sensitization
Sleep-related complications including untreated obstructive sleep apnea, which independently raises cardiovascular and stroke risk and amplifies fibromyalgia symptoms

supportResources.name: National Fibromyalgia Association
supportResources.description: US patient organization offering education, advocacy, and community resources.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Fibromyalgia Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that develops fibromyalgia diagnostic criteria.

supportResources.name: CDC — Fibromyalgia
supportResources.description: US public health agency overview with epidemiology and self-management guidance.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation — Fibromyalgia
supportResources.description: Patient community, lifestyle resources, and clinical updates from a leading US patient advocacy group.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ME/CFS centers on post-exertional malaise — disproportionate worsening of symptoms 12-48 hours after even minor exertion — and severe unrelieved fatigue, with pain often secondary. Fibromyalgia is primarily a widespread pain disorder with fatigue. The two overlap in roughly 50% of patients and share central sensitization mechanisms, but graded exercise is first-line in fibromyalgia and must be approached with great caution in ME/CFS where it can trigger lasting setbacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis causes objective joint swelling, warmth, and morning stiffness lasting hours, with elevated CRP and ESR, positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP, and erosions on imaging. Fibromyalgia produces widespread soft-tissue pain with normal inflammatory markers and no joint damage. The two coexist in about 20-30% of RA patients and recognizing the overlap prevents over-treatment with biologics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyalgia rheumatica is an inflammatory disease of older adults (age 50+, peak 70-75) producing aching and stiffness in the shoulder and hip girdles with markedly elevated ESR and CRP and dramatic response to low-dose prednisone within days. Fibromyalgia symptoms are widespread, inflammatory markers are normal, and prednisone does not help.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus is a systemic autoimmune disease with objective findings including photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, alopecia, serositis, nephritis, hematologic abnormalities, and consistently positive antinuclear antibodies at meaningful titer. Fibromyalgia produces pain and fatigue without organ involvement and with normal laboratory findings. Low-titer ANA in a healthy-appearing woman with widespread pain alone does not change a fibromyalgia diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myofascial Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myofascial pain syndrome is a regional muscle pain disorder defined by trigger points within taut bands of muscle producing referred pain. It is localized rather than widespread, responds to manual therapy and trigger-point injection, and lacks the systemic fatigue, cognitive symptoms, and sleep disturbance of fibromyalgia. The two often coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares central sensitization mechanisms and overlaps clinically in roughly half of patients; differentiated by post-exertional malaise being central in ME/CFS.

relatedConditions.name: Myofascial Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Regional muscle pain with trigger points that often coexists with fibromyalgia; treated with manual therapy and trigger-point injection in addition to fibromyalgia care.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Concomitant fibromyalgia is present in 20-30% of RA patients and is the commonest reason for persistent symptoms despite well-controlled inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Affects roughly 30% of fibromyalgia patients; bidirectional relationship with pain. Dual-action antidepressants treat both domains.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Affects 40-70% of fibromyalgia patients; another nociplastic disorder driven by central sensitization.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia a real disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fibromyalgia is a recognized chronic disorder of central pain processing, classified under ICD-10 as M79.7 and supported by functional MRI evidence of altered pain pathways, cerebrospinal fluid studies showing elevated substance P and glutamate, and three FDA-approved medications. It is real, biological, and treatable.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: The core symptoms are widespread pain across both sides of the body lasting at least three months, profound fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive difficulty known as fibro fog, and heightened sensitivity to pressure, light, sound, and temperature. Headaches, irritable bowel symptoms, anxiety, and depression frequently coexist.

faqs.question: How is fibromyalgia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is a clinical diagnosis made using the 2016 ACR criteria, which require widespread pain in at least four of five body regions, symptom duration of three months or more, and a Widespread Pain Index combined with Symptom Severity Score above a defined threshold. Blood tests and imaging exclude conditions that mimic fibromyalgia rather than confirm it.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia an autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: No. Fibromyalgia is not an autoimmune disease. It is classified as a nociplastic pain disorder, meaning the underlying problem is altered pain processing in the central nervous system rather than immune-driven inflammation or tissue damage. Inflammatory markers and autoantibody tests are typically normal in primary fibromyalgia.

faqs.question: What causes fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is caused by central sensitization — the brain and spinal cord amplifying ordinary sensory signals into pain. Contributing factors include genetic predisposition (heritability around 50%), triggering events such as whiplash, surgery, or severe infection, disordered sleep, sustained stress, and adverse childhood experiences. No single cause explains every case.

faqs.question: Why do so many more women get fibromyalgia than men?
faqs.answer: Under the older 1990 ACR criteria the female-to-male ratio was about 9 to 1, partly because the criteria emphasized tender points that women report more readily. Under the 2016 criteria the ratio is closer to 3 to 1. True biological differences in pain processing, hormonal modulation, and care-seeking behavior all contribute.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get diagnosed with fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: The average delay from symptom onset to fibromyalgia diagnosis is 2.3 to 5 years in international cohorts. Delay is driven by intermittent symptoms, normal investigations, multiple specialty referrals, and patchy clinician familiarity with current criteria. An early confident diagnosis improves outcomes by reducing unnecessary testing and starting effective treatment sooner.

faqs.question: Can fibromyalgia be cured?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is not curable in the strict sense, but it is highly treatable and many patients achieve sustained remission. With multimodal treatment combining exercise, behavioral therapy, sleep optimization, and medication, roughly 25% reach remission and another 30-50% achieve meaningful long-term symptom reduction. The condition does not progress to joint or organ damage.

faqs.question: What is the most effective treatment for fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Graded aerobic exercise has the largest evidence base of any single treatment and is recommended as first-line by every modern guideline. Cognitive behavioral therapy and structured patient education are added from diagnosis. When medication is needed, duloxetine, pregabalin, milnacipran, and low-dose amitriptyline have the strongest randomized-trial support.

faqs.question: Which medication is best for fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: There is no single best medication. Duloxetine, pregabalin, and milnacipran hold FDA approval, with response rates of roughly 35-50% versus 25-30% for placebo. Choice depends on dominant symptoms: duloxetine when depression coexists, pregabalin when sleep and anxiety are prominent, milnacipran when fatigue dominates. Low-dose amitriptyline is a first-line alternative outside the US.

faqs.question: Should I take opioids for fibromyalgia pain?
faqs.answer: No. The American College of Rheumatology, EULAR, NICE, and the CDC all recommend against long-term opioids for fibromyalgia. Opioids provide minimal pain benefit, worsen central sensitization over time, and add substantial risks of dependence, hyperalgesia, and death. Tramadol can be used short term in selected patients but is not a long-term strategy.

faqs.question: Does fibromyalgia get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia does not progress in a structural sense — there is no joint, muscle, or organ damage. Symptom severity fluctuates over time and depends on treatment, stress, sleep, fitness, and comorbid conditions. With effective multimodal treatment many patients improve steadily over 6-24 months. Untreated, symptoms tend to persist or worsen during stress, illness, or inactivity.

faqs.question: Does exercise make fibromyalgia worse?
faqs.answer: Started too aggressively, yes — many patients have had a discouraging experience. Started low and built up gradually, exercise is the single most effective long-term treatment for fibromyalgia. Begin with 5-10 minutes of low-impact activity such as walking or pool exercise, and accept brief temporary flares as a normal part of adaptation rather than a sign of harm.

faqs.question: What is fibro fog?
faqs.answer: Fibro fog describes the cognitive symptoms of fibromyalgia — reduced working memory, slowed processing speed, difficulty finding words, impaired concentration, and momentary lapses in attention. It is a real, measurable component of the disorder rather than a sign of dementia. Fibro fog worsens with pain, poor sleep, and stress, and improves when those drivers are addressed.

faqs.question: Can children get fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile fibromyalgia is diagnosed most often in adolescents aged 13-15 with female predominance. Childhood-onset disease accounts for under 5% of cases. Prognosis is more favorable than adult-onset disease, though up to 50% continue to meet criteria into adulthood. Treatment principles mirror adult care with exercise, cognitive behavioral therapy, and selective medication use.

faqs.question: Does fibromyalgia run in families?
faqs.answer: Yes. First-degree relatives of patients with fibromyalgia have approximately an 8-fold increased risk. Heritability is estimated at around 50% from twin and family studies. Multiple genes related to pain processing and neurotransmitter pathways contribute, including variants in COMT and the serotonin transporter, but no single gene causes the disease.

faqs.question: What is the difference between fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia is primarily a widespread pain disorder with fatigue as a major secondary feature. Chronic fatigue syndrome is primarily a fatigue disorder defined by post-exertional malaise — disproportionate symptom worsening 12-48 hours after exertion. The two overlap in roughly half of patients but graded exercise is first-line in fibromyalgia and must be approached cautiously in ME/CFS.

faqs.question: Is fibromyalgia the same as arthritis?
faqs.answer: No. Arthritis involves inflammation or damage within the joints, often with swelling, warmth, and abnormal imaging. Fibromyalgia is a central nervous system pain-processing disorder without joint damage, joint swelling, or imaging abnormalities. Fibromyalgia can coexist with arthritis — about 20-30% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis also meet fibromyalgia criteria.

faqs.question: Does fibromyalgia cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Fibromyalgia itself does not directly cause weight gain, but pain, fatigue, and disrupted sleep often reduce physical activity and disturb appetite regulation, leading to gradual weight gain in many patients. Some medications — pregabalin and amitriptyline in particular — can add 2-5 kg over months. Gradual aerobic exercise and balanced nutrition reverse this trend in most patients.

faqs.question: Can I work with fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: Most patients can work, often with adjustments such as ergonomic seating, flexible hours, scheduled breaks, and the ability to alternate posture. Roughly 75% of patients remain employed with appropriate treatment and accommodations. A minority develop disabling symptoms that limit work; early diagnosis and structured treatment substantially reduce that risk.

faqs.question: How much does fibromyalgia treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic duloxetine, amitriptyline, and cyclobenzaprine cost roughly USD 5-20 per month in most countries. Pregabalin is available generically at USD 10-30 monthly. Cognitive behavioral therapy and graded exercise programs vary widely; group programs and digital CBT platforms are often the most cost-effective options.

faqs.question: When should I see a rheumatologist for fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: See a rheumatologist when the diagnosis is uncertain, when inflammatory or autoimmune disease cannot be confidently excluded, when there is concomitant rheumatic disease driving part of the symptom picture, or when first-line treatments at adequate doses have failed. Well-managed fibromyalgia without diagnostic uncertainty can be followed effectively in primary care.

metaDescription: Fibromyalgia: widespread pain, fatigue, and fibro fog from altered central pain processing. Symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed by our medical board.
